coredata.dc:title: MIL-53(Fe) with immobilized sulfo-modified carbon quantum dots as a novel and dual-function Brønsted-Lewis acid catalyst for the synthesis of tetrazoles and triazoles and their in vitro evaluation as antibacterial agents
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ghasemzadeh, Mohammad Ali
Mirhosseini-Eshkevari, Boshra
Javadi, Ali
coredata.dc:description: In the present paper, we prepared NiFe2O4@CA@MIL53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanocomposite by immobilizing sulfo-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-SO3H) on the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe) nanoparticles. The mesoporous catalyst of functionalized carbon quantum dots were characterized by several techniques viz; FT-IR, XRD, SEM, EDX/mapping, BET, TGA, and VSM. NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H as a novel and high-performance heterogeneous nanocomposite was applied as an exceptional catalyst for the preparation of some 5-substituted-1H-tetrazole and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazole derivatives by using variable aldehydes, sodium azide, and hydroxylamine hydrochloride or nitromethane under solvent conditions. Particularly, valuable features of this protocol are short reaction times, mild conditions, considerable yields, reusability of catalyst, simple work-up, and atom efficiency. Furthermore, the antibacterial behavior of the prepared compounds was scrutinized beside gram-negativeand gram-positive bacteria (Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus) and the obtained results illustrated appropriate effects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Metal-organic frameworks
Tetrazoles
Triazoles
Nanocomposite
Heterogeneous catalyst
Heterocycle
Magnetic
Multi-component reactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General preparation of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles (4a-l) and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazoles (6a-l) using NiFe2O4 @CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanocomposite
Recycling and reusing of the catalyst

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Financial Support was provided by the Research Affairs Office of the Qom Medical Sciences, Islamic Azad University, Qom, I. R. Iran [grant number: 7214].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Drugs and bioactive molecules containing tetrazole moiety.
FT-IR spectrum of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
XRD pattern of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
FE-SEM images, EDX spectrum, and elemental mapping analysis of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
BET-plot (a), Adsorption/desorption (b), BJH-plot (c) of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
TGA profile of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
Magnetization curves of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe) (a), and NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H (b).
The influence of different catalysts on the model study.
The influence of various solvents on the preparation of 5-(4-nitrophenyl)-1H-tetrazole (4f).
Reusability of the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H catalyst.
The XRD pattern (a) and SEM image (b) of the recovered NiFe2O4@CA@MIL 53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H after 5 runs.
The antibacterial experiment results for the disk bearing E. coli and the disk related to S. aureus bacteria.
Preparation of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazoles using NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
Synthesis steps for the preparation of NiFe2O4 @CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanocomposite.
The model reaction for the synthesis of 5-(4-nitrophenyl)-1H-tetrazole (4f).
Proposed mechanism pathway for synthesizing 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles using NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
Proposed mechanism for synthesizing 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazoles using NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H.
Effect of catalyst loading and diverse temperatures on the model study. a

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of catalytic activity of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanostructure with some other catalysts for the synthesis of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Based on the reaction of sodium azide, hydroxylamine hydrochloride, and aldehyde.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The antibacterial activities of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazole and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazole derivatives.

head.ce:title: MIL-53(Fe) with immobilized sulfo-modified carbon quantum dots as a novel and dual-function Brønsted-Lewis acid catalyst for the synthesis of tetrazoles and triazoles and their in vitro evaluation as antibacterial agents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Qo.C., Islamic Azad University, Qom, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Qo.C., Islamic Azad University, Qom, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Medical Sciences, QoMS.C., Islamic Azad University, Qom, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Medical Sciences, QoMS.C., Islamic Azad University, Qom, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present paper, we prepared NiFe2O4@CA@MIL53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanocomposite by immobilizing sulfo-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-SO3H) on the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe) nanoparticles. The mesoporous catalyst of functionalized carbon quantum dots were characterized by several techniques viz; FT-IR, XRD, SEM, EDX/mapping, BET, TGA, and VSM. NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H as a novel and high-performance heterogeneous nanocomposite was applied as an exceptional catalyst for the preparation of some 5-substituted-1H-tetrazole and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazole derivatives by using variable aldehydes, sodium azide, and hydroxylamine hydrochloride or nitromethane under solvent conditions. Particularly, valuable features of this protocol are short reaction times, mild conditions, considerable yields, reusability of catalyst, simple work-up, and atom efficiency. Furthermore, the antibacterial behavior of the prepared compounds was scrutinized beside gram-negativeand gram-positive bacteria (Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus) and the obtained results illustrated appropriate effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots are characterized by intriguing characteristics, including favorable environmental sustainability, photostability, easy synthesis, high dispersibility in water, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and low production costs [1,2]. Based on the same properties, carbon quantum dots are regarded as superb candidates to create optical nanoprobes for biosensors [3], chemical analysis [4], fluorescent labeling and bioimaging [5,6], and fingerprint imaging [7]. Given that a variety of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) materials (ZIF, MIL, HUST, UiO, IRMOF, PCN, CPL, etc.) were introduced, which are characterized by a significant specific surface area, ordered porous structures, rich active sites, these materials have become the focus of scholars’ investigations [8]. Due to the unique structure of metal–organic frameworks, they are regarded as ideal materials used to combine with other functional materials [9]. In general, a metal–organic framework material cannot meet the sophisticated requirements. Also, as a novel research hot spot, preparing quantum dots-metal–organic frameworks (QDs-MOFs) composites has received considerable attention recently [10]. The quantum dots featuring high surface energy and small sizes are inclined to aggregate, which leads to declined performance. The QDs-MOFs combination can improve the stability and dispersion characteristics of quantum dots. Furthermore, QDs-MOF composites are characterized by the advantages attributable to both MOF and QDs compounds and are capable of endowing materials with new functions as a result of the synergistic effects [11,12].

In general, antimicrobial agents are regarded as an important and effective solution to eliminate microbial infections and control bacterial growth on the surfaces of materials. Based on the Gram staining technique, one can categorize bacteria into Gram-negative (G−) and Gram-positive (G+) categories. In this technique, iodine and the amethyst dye are used to treat the bacteria, the staining characteristics of which are then assessed based on the rhamnocytidylic acid and cytosolic polysaccharides found in their cellular walls. Based on this classification, the bacteria are divided into distinct categories, each of which displays noticeable differences in resistance to drugs, pathogenic tendencies, and growth characteristics [13]. As a result, there is a global tendency to obtain extremely effective antimicrobial agents characterized by the minimum side effects and broad-spectrum activity.

The derivatives of tetrazole and triazole, which are known for growing applications in many scientific areas, are among the key heterocyclic compounds that contain N as a heteroatom. These heterocycles have attracted great attention of many researchers, which is attributed to their widespread applications in chemistry, pharmacology, medicinal industries, biology, etc. [14]. A great number of drugs contain tetrazolyl moieties, e.g., cis zolarsartan [15] valsartan, candesartan, and losartan [16,17]. Besides their use in drugs, a variety of biological activities have been attributed to tetrazole and its derivatives, e.g., anti-cancer [18], anti-fungal [19], anti-tubercular [20], anti-allergic [21], anti-bacterial [22], and in particular anti-HIV [23] activities. In Fig. 1 , a number of the drugs containing 2H- or 1H-tetrazole substituents (Valastran, candesartan, cefmenoxime, cefotiam, pemirolast, losartan) have been presented. These drugs are characterized by antimicrobial, hypertensive, anti-allergic, anti-viral, nootropic, cytostatic, and other biological activities. A group of methods has been described in the literature for the preparation of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles from aldehydes. The utilized catalysts included Cu(OAc)2 [24], TiCl3 [25], (NH4)4Ce(SO4)4·2H2O [26], Cu-MCM-41 [27], I2 [28], Humic acid [29], P2O5 [30], and [bmim]N3/Cu(OAc)2 [31]. Moreover, a great number of approaches have been presented to synthesize 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazoles from alkenyl bromides by employing Cu [32] and Pd [33] and heterogeneous catalysts [34]. Even though all the above-mentioned techniques have their own merits, most of them suffer from certain drawbacks, like toxic organic solvents, high temperatures, and the use of commercially unavailable catalysts. To avoid the above disadvantages, it is necessary to develop more efficient and simpler protocols. In the contemporary era, one-pot multi-component reactions (MCRs) have become the focus of considerable attention to synthesize various complexes and contribute to bearable through simplification of the synthetic pathways [35].

To continue our attempts to conduct novel synthetic techniques for different heterocycles by employing nanostructures [36–40], we planned and fabricated a novel and economical magnetic NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H as a Brønsted and Lewis acid catalyst for the synthesis of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazoles from cheap and simply accessible starting material aldehydes along with sodium azide and hydroxylamine hydrochloride or nitromethane. The prepared heterogeneous composite includes several advanced materials, including nickel ferrite (NiFe2O4), citric acid (CA), MIL-53(Fe), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and sulfonic acid groups (SO3H), resulting in a multifunctional catalyst with remarkable properties. The incorporation of NiFe2O4 provides magnetic properties that enable easy separation and reuse of the catalyst. While citric acid offers structural stability and enhances the material’s dispersibility in various solvents. MIL-53(Fe), a metal–organic framework, contributes to high surface area and porosity, promoting better access to reactive sites. Furthermore, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with their unique electronic properties cause to enhance the catalytic activity. Together, the MOF@CQDs nanocomposite offers a synergistic effect that significantly boosts catalytic performance. The catalytic process using the MOF@CQDs nanocomposite is characterized by its high selectivity, excellent reusability, and rapid reaction rates, outperforming traditional catalysts regarding reaction efficiency and sustainability. Additionally, the sulfonic acid (SO3H) groups attached to the composite serve as acidic sites, which are crucial for accelerating the cyclization reactions needed for tetrazoles and triazoles synthesis. The combined synergy of these components results in a highly efficient, eco-friendly catalyst that not only promotes the synthesis of these heterocyclic compounds but also ensures high yields with excellent selectivity. Moreover, the catalyst shows good reusability and stability, which makes it a promising candidate for sustainable green chemistry applications [41–46].

To evaluate the reusability of the nanocatalyst, the magnetic catalyst was detached from the mixture by an external magnet and was reused for up to at least five recycles. Furthermore, some compounds were subsequently evaluated for their antimicrobial activities (Scheme 1 ).

Initially, the magnetic nanoparticles of NiFe2O4 were prepared using the co-precipitation technique reported by Zhanget al. [47]. Then, 0.5 g of the synthesized NiFe2O4 NPs was sonicated in a citric acid solution (100 mL) for 30 min. Later, the mixture was mechanically stirred for 5 h to form NiFe2O4@CA particles. Lastly, the prepared NiFe2O4@CA was washed with water and ethanol many times, and dried under vacuum at 50 °C.

Firstly, the citric acid-functionalized NiFe2O4 (0.1 g) was dispersed into DMF solution (25 mL) of FeCl3·6H2O (0.189 g) and H2BDC (0.049 g) under ultrasound irradiation for 15 min. Next, the mixture was put in an autoclave at 170 °C for 18 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was filtered and washed by DMF several times and then dried under vacuum at 50 °C, which was denoted as NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe).

Through a facile hydrothermal synthesis by following the previous report, CQDs were prepared [48]. An amount of 2 g of red lentils was rinsed twice using the deionized water, and then dried at room temperature. Using a mortar and pestle, it was converted into a fine powder and mixed with a 40 mL volume of deionized water for 30 min at room temperature. Next, the solution was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave and put in an oven at a temperature of 200 °C for five hours. Eventually, the mixture was centrifuged to remove the undissolved and larger particles, and the supernatant solution was collected, and passed through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Finally, the resulting precipitate was dried at 60 °C for 5 h.

CQDs-SO3H particles were obtained by sulfonation of carbon quantum dots using a solution of chlorosulfonic acid in dichloromethane [49]. The typical procedure was as follows: CQDs (1 g) were sonicated (15 min at 0 °C) in dried dichloromethane (10 mL). Subsequently, 1 mL of chlorosulfonic acid was mixed with a 10 mL volume of dried dichloromethane, which was then added to the mixture in a dropwise manner. This was followed by stirring the solution for 15 min to exhaust all gases from the solution. Next, the solution was stirred at room temperature for eight hours. Eventually, after centrifuging CQDs-SO3H at 6000 rpm for 10 min, it was rinsed several times using dried CH2Cl2 and then dried for 12 h at a temperature of 50 °C in an oven.

An impregnation technique was used to prepare the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanostructure. CQDs-SO3H were immobilized onto the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe) surfaces. Scheme 2 shows the schematic preparation. After dispersing of 0.1 g the as-synthesized NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe) in 20 mL of deionized water, 5 mL of the CQDs-SO3H solution was added to the suspension in a dropwise manner. The resulting mixture was subjected to sonication for 20 min in an ice bath. Then, using a magnetic stirrer, the suspension was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. After obtaining the final product using centrifugation, it was rinsed using deionized water three times and then dried in a vacuum oven for 12 h at a temperature of 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General preparation of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles (4a-l) and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazoles (6a-l) using NiFe2O4 @CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: In a round bottom flask, dimethylformamide (5 mL) was added to a solution of sodium azide (1 mmol), aldehyde (1 mmol), hydroxylamine hydrochloride or nitromethane (1 mmol), and NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H (0.007 g) the catalyst. The resulting reaction mixture was heated at a temperature of 110 °C (Table 2). Once the reaction was completed, as indicated by TLC, the resulting mixture was cooled down to room temperature, and an external magnet was used to separate the catalyst. The catalyst was washed with distilled water and dried for further use. To achieve the products, the solvent was evaporated by rotary, and the obtained residue was crystallized from ethanol. The obtained data related to the synthesized products are shown in Table 2. The FT-IR, 1H NMR, and 13C NMR spectroscopies were used to confirm the structures of the products.
Spectral data of the new products are given below.

The antibacterial assays of the heterocycles were carried out by the CLSI guidelines [50] and incubated for 24 h at a temperature of 37 °C, by employing the good diffusion technique on Mueller-Hinton Agar (MHA), the prepared products were evaluated in terms of their antibacterial activity. The reported inhibitory zones were in millimeters. The literature for the antibacterial behavior of the synthesized heterocycles was E. coli (ATCC 25922) and S. aureus (ATCC 25923). Mueller-Hinton Agar plates were inoculated using a bacterial strain subject to aseptic conditions. All of the products had a 512 μg/mL concentration in solvent. As soon as the incubation was completed, the growth inhibition zone was determined.

The structure of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H as a bifunctional catalyst was fully recognized using several methods such as FT-IR, XRD, SEM, EDX/mapping, BET, TGA, and VSM analysis. The NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H was used for preparing some 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazoles.

FT-IR technique was qualitatively used to identify different functional groups in the as-synthesized NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H catalyst (Fig. 2 ). The existence of Ni-O and Fe-O bonds in the structure was verified by the characteristic of a broad peak that appeared at 602 cm−1
[51]. The absorption band at 1682 cm−1 is qualified for the stretching vibration of carbonyl groups (C=O) on the CA and CQDs. The broad absorption band around 3443 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching of hydroxyl groups (O–H) on the CA [52], CQDs, and −SO3H moieties [49]. These bonds are comparable to those in the H2BDC linker in the MIL-53(Fe) structure [53]. The peaks around 752 cm−1 and 1286 cm−1 are related to the vibrations of S-O (stretching vibrations) and S=O (asymmetric stretching vibrations) bonds in the −SO3H group, respectively [49].

The XRD pattern of the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3Hnanoparticles is shown in Fig. 3 . The peaks at 26°, 28°, 29.1°, 30.2°, 32°, 41.5° and 50.1° (2θ°) indicate the formation of the NiFe2O4 nanoparticles [54].Also, the peaks at 8.2°, 14.1°, 16.1° 17°, 20° and 22° (2θ°) display the existence of the MIL-53(Fe) [43].Moreover, it can be seen that the XRD pattern showed the peak at 2θ = 25° that can be assigned to CQDs-SO3H [55]. All the diffraction peaks could be indexed to (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1), (4 4 0) and (5 3 3), (6 0 0), (6 1 0), (6 3 3), (6 4 5) and (6 5 0) planes (Fig. 3). The attendance of sharp peaks at the XRD pattern infers the crystallinity of the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3Hnanostructure.

Fig. 4 illustrates the FE-SEM images of the as-prepared magnetic catalyst. The hydrogen bonds between −OSO3H groups in NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H lead to agglomeration of nanocomposite. The average particle size of the nanostructure is about 25–50 nm, which demonstrates a quite agreement with XRD analysis.

Elemental mapping analysis shows C, O, Fe, Ni, and S atoms, confirming the structure of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H (Fig. 4). Besides, the well-dispersed scattering of elements in the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H was verified by this analysis (Fig. 4).

The textural attribute of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H was also studied by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 5 a,b). A hysteresis loop is detected indicating the existence of mesoporous in the composite [56]. The calculated surface areas for nanostructure and total pore volumes were 17.76 m2g−1 and 4.018 cm3g−1, respectively. The pore size distribution of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H based on the BJH technique is presented in Fig. 5c. This plot clearly shows pores diameter of the nanocomposite was 1.64 nm.

The thermal stability of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H was investigated by employing a TGA experiment. Fig. 6 illustrates three main phases of weight loss. The first step exhibited a weight loss of 3 wt% that is ascribable to the loss of adsorbed solvent molecules below 240 °C. The second step displayed a weight loss of about 17 wt% from 240 °C to 400 °C, caused by the thermal decomposition of CA and MIL-53(Fe) [53]. Finally, the degradation observed at 400–540 °C (23 wt%) can be attributed to the destruction of CQDs −OSO3H [49].

The magnetic properties of the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe) and NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H were calculated by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). As demonstrated in Fig. 7 , the magnetic saturation (MS) values of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe) and NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanocomposites are 46.2 and 31.6 emug−1, respectively (Fig. 7a and 7b). The slightly lower MS value of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H mostly a consequence of the incorporation of CQDs-SO3H. The satisfactory magnetic properties allow NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H to be rapidly and efficiently detached from the media by a magnet.

After successful production and characterization of the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H, it was used to prepare some 5-substituted-1H-tetrazole and 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazole derivatives. Firstly, we selected the reaction of 4-nitrobenzaldehyde, sodium azide, and hydroxylamine hydrochloride as a model reaction, assessing the best reaction conditions (Scheme 3 ). The model reaction was tested using different catalysts, solvents, and temperatures.

This investigation is important due to the amazing catalytic activity of our presented catalyst in comparison with other catalysts, e.g., CuI, NiFe2O4, MIL-53(Fe), and NiFe2O4@CA. The model reaction was studied separately for each of the above catalysts (0.007 g). The best result for the synthesis of 5-(4-nitrophenyl)-1H-tetrazole (4f) with a yield of 98 % was obtained within 40 min using NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H as catalyst and dimethylformamide as solvent at a temperature of 110 °C (Fig. 8 ).

In continue, the model study was performed in the presence of a variety of solvents, including acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, dimethylformamide, ethanol, toluene, and water, as well as free solvent conditions to study the effect of the solvent conditions (Fig. 9 ). Among the various solvents tested, the best solvent considering the yield and time of the intended product was selected as dimethylformamide (98 % yield within 40 min).

In the following, the effect of various quantities of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H has been investigated in the model study at various temperatures in the range of 50–120 °C (Table 1 ). The best results were associated with an amount of 0.007 g of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL@CQDs-SO3H at a temperature of 110 °C.

When the reaction conditions were optimized, the effectiveness and applicability of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H were studied for the preparation some of 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazole and 5-substituted-1H-tetrazole derivatives. As indicated by Table 2 , different aldehydes containing electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups, sodium azide, and hydroxylamine hydrochloride or nitromethane as starting materials afforded desired products (4a–4l) and (6a–6l) in good to outstanding yields (82–98 %) in short reaction times (40–130 min.).

Evaluation of the catalytic performance of some heterogeneous catalysts described in the literature and the present study for the production of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles are shown in Table 3 . As shown from the obtained data in Table 3, NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H is the best catalyst for the production of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles.

Based on previous literature [24,26] together with experimental results, a plausible mechanism for the preparation of 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles is suggested (Scheme 4 ). NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H likely acts as a Brønsted-Lewis acid catalyst due to Lewis sites on the NiF2O4 and MIL-53(Fe) moieties beside Bronsted acidic sites related to the citric acids and sulfonic groups linked to CQDs. After the activation of the carbonyl group of benzaldehyde by a catalyst, the nucleophilic attack of the nitrogen atom of hydroxylamine occurred to achieve the benzaldehyde oxime (A). Then, NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H readily activated the C=N bond by co-coordinating of benzaldehyde oxime. This might simplify the [3 + 2] cycloaddition between azide ion and the C=N bond of benzaldehyde oxime which results in the formation of intermediate (B), and eventually elimination of water affords to produce 5-phenyl-1H-tetrazoles.

Furthermore, a reasonable mechanism for preparing 5-substituted-1H-tetrazoles based on our knowledge and a previous study is provided in Scheme 5
[60]. Firstly, nitroolefin (A) is produced by the reaction of aromatic aldehyde with nitromethane. The catalytic reaction is then carried out by the coordination of nitroolefin on the surface of the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H catalyst, followed by the nucleophilic addition of sodium azide, which results in the formation of intermediate B. Next, intermediate B undergoes the cyclization, and HNO2 is eliminated, which leads to the formation of the desired 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazole product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recycling and reusing of the catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The recyclability and reusability of the NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H as a catalyst were also studied on a model study under optimized reaction conditions. The outcomes are shown in Fig. 10 . NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H can be reused up to five runs in the reaction without a significant reduction in product yield.

The chemical structure of recovered NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H was verified using XRD pattern and SEM image. There is no significant difference between the XRD and SEM image of the fresh and recovered nanocomposite (Fig. 11 a, b). Also, the acid sites (1.24 mmol/g) of the catalyst after five times reused had no dramatic changes based on the acid-base titration measurement, in comparison with acid sites of the fresh NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H nanocomposite (1.27 mmol/g). These facts prove that the efficiency, appearance and structure of NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H catalyst remained intact in the recycling process and there was no considerable deformation or leaching after 5 runs.

In conclusion, the newly developed NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H composite catalyst proves to be highly effective for the synthesis of 4-aryl-NH-1,2,3-triazole and 5-substituted-1H-tetrazole derivatives under mild and eco-friendly conditions. The catalyst was synthesized and optimized to yield the desired heterocyclic compounds in excellent yields ranging from 82 % to 98 % and with short reaction times between 40 and 120 min. The NiFe2O4@CA@MIL-53(Fe)@CQDs-SO3H catalyst demonstrated exceptional recyclability, maintaining over 90 % of its initial catalytic activity after five consecutive runs, thus ensuring the process’s cost-effectiveness. The antibacterial evaluation of the synthesized derivatives showed promising results, with several compounds exhibiting inhibition zones of 13–25 mm against the tested bacterial strains. Overall, the proposed catalytic system offers a robust, efficient, and sustainable method for the rapid synthesis of bioactive heterocyclic compounds, combining mild reaction conditions, high yields, and a simple work-up process.

Mohammad Ali Ghasemzadeh: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Boshra Mirhosseini-Eshkevari: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Data curation. Ali Javadi: Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Financial Support was provided by the Research Affairs Office of the Qom Medical Sciences, Islamic Azad University, Qom, I. R. Iran [grant number: 7214].

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mohammad Ali Ghasemzadeh received his B.Sc., and M.Sc. degrees in Chemistry and Organic Chemistry from the University of Kashan (Iran) in 2005 and 2008, respectively. He completed his Ph.D. degree in Organic Chemistry from University of Kashan, under the supervision of Professor Javad Safaei-Ghomi in 2012. He then joined the Department of Chemistry at Islamic Azad University (Qom Branch, Iran) as an Assistant Professor, where he and his group are now based. His research group specializes in functionalized metal nanostructures, metal-organic frameworks, and supported ionic liquids as catalysts for the synthesis of heterocyclic compounds via multicomponent reactions.
